en.extract:
The worm-eating warbler (Helmitheros vermivorum) is a small New World warbler that breeds in the Eastern United States and migrates to southern Mexico, the Caribbean, and Central America for the winter. It is the only species in the genus Helmitheros.


Taxonomy
The worm-eating warbler was formally described in 1789 by the German naturalist Johann Friedrich Gmelin in his revised and expanded edition of Carl Linnaeus's Systema Naturae. He placed it with the wagtails in the genus Motacilla and coined the binomial name Motacilla vermivora. Gmelin based his account on the "worm-eater" that had been described and illustrated in 1760 by the English naturalist George Edwards in his Gleanings of Natural History. Edwards had received a specimen from the American naturalist William Bartram that had been collected in Pennsylvania. The worm-eating warbler is now placed in the genus Helmitheros that was introduced in 1819 by the French polymath Constantine Samuel Rafinesque. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek helmins meaning "worm", and -thēras meaning "hunter". The specific epithet vermivorum is from Latin vermis meaning "worm" and -vorus meaning "-eating". The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
The worm-eating warbler is a small New World warbler. It is around 13 cm (5.1 in) in length and weighs 11.8–17.4 g (0.42–0.61 oz). It is relatively plain with olive-brown upperparts and light-coloured underparts, but has black and light brown stripes on its head. It has a slim pointed bill and pink legs. In immature birds, the head stripes are brownish. The male's song is a short high-pitched trill. This bird's call is a chip or tseet. Worm-eating warblers are sexually monomorphic. Males and females can only reliably be sexed during the breeding season by the presence of a brood patch in females or a cloacal protuberance in males. These birds are also difficult to age. Hatch year/second year birds have rusty tips on tertials that wear off by March of the following year. Juveniles can be distinguished by duskier head markings, and cinnamon wingbars.


Distribution and habitat
These birds breed in the Eastern United States. Their selected habitats vary significantly between populations. In much of their range, worm-eating warblers are associated with mature hardwood forests on steep slopes. However, recent attention has been focused on coastal breeding populations, as little is known about their ecology or status. Historically, coastal populations would select for pocosin ecosystems. More recently, however, these populations have shifted to frequent use of pine plantations. Current use of pine plantations has resulted in densities higher than in areas previously thought to be their natural habitat. This shift in habitat selection likely demonstrates that worm-eating warblers are more closely associated with shrub structure than stand age or size. If this is the case, the landscape changes that occurred on the Atlantic coastal plain may have had less of an impact on these birds than previously described. Maintaining this species habitat may require managing for dense shrubby midstory and understory. Due to their reliance on shrub structure for foraging, and ground nesting behavior, frequent fires have a negative impact on this species. Other management strategies that reduce the shrub mid-story, increase herbaceous growth, and decrease canopy cover are likely to have a similar effect. More information is needed about their breeding habits in coastal regions as these forests are likely to represent different conditions from their inland counterparts. Fat deposits play a key role in allowing for long-distance migration in most passerines. Stopover habitat, or areas that allow birds to replenish their fat stores, are also critical.
In winter, these birds migrate to southern Mexico, the Greater Antilles, and Central America particularly along the Caribbean Slope where they occupy both scrub and moist forests. Worm-eating warblers have disappeared from some parts of their range due to habitat loss but their ability to use both scrub and moist forest ecosystems may be beneficial to the long term conservation of this species.


Behaviour and ecology


Breeding
This bird breeds in dense deciduous forests in the eastern United States, usually on wooded slopes. The nest is an open cup placed on the ground, hidden among dead leaves. It is one of several species of new-world warblers that nests on the ground including the black-and-white warbler (Mniotilta varia), the ovenbird (Seiurus aurocapilla), the northern waterthrush (Parkesia noveboracensis), Louisiana waterthrush (Parkesia motacilla), palm warbler (Setophaga palmarum), and the Kentucky warbler (Geothlypis formosa). The female lays four or five eggs. Both parents feed the young; they may try to distract predators near the nest by pretending to be injured. Worm-eating warblers are often parasitized by brown-headed cowbirds (Molothrus ater) where forest fragmentation occurs. Reducing forest fragmentation may prove vital if populations of worm-eating warblers suffer large declines.


Food and feeding
The diet of worm-eating warblers varies across habitat types. This variation can be attributed to different predator avoidance strategies employed by common prey items. On their breeding grounds, worm-eating warblers glean mostly from live foliage, searching for arthropods. On the wintering grounds, this species gleans insects almost exclusively from dead plant material. The name "worm-eating" refers to the numerous Lepidopteran larvae that this species consumes; they rarely if ever eat earthworms.
Use of pesticides, especially those broadcast over a wide area, is likely to have an effect on most insectivorous songbird species, including the worm-eating warbler. These pesticides decrease the species' primary food source and could result in long-term toxicity.


References


External links
Species account - Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Species account - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification

(Gmelin, 1789) }} The '''worm-eating warbler''' ('''''Helmitheros vermivorum''''') is a small [[New World warbler]] that breeds in the Eastern United States and [[bird migration|migrate]]s to southern [[Mexico]], the [[Caribbean]], and [[Central America]] for the winter. It is the only species in the genus '''''Helmitheros.''''' ==Taxonomy== The worm-eating warbler was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1789 by the German naturalist [[Johann Friedrich Gmelin]] in his revised and expanded edition of [[Carl Linnaeus]]'s ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He placed it with the wagtails in the [[genus]] ''[[Motacilla]]'' and coined the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Motacilla vermivora''.{{ cite book | last=Gmelin | first=Johann Friedrich | author-link=Johann Friedrich Gmelin| year=1789 | title=Systema naturae per regna tria naturae: secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=13th | volume=1, Part 2 | language=Latin | location=Lipsiae [Leipzig] | publisher=Georg. Emanuel. De 70 nouveaux Genres d'Animaux découverts dans l'intérieur des États-Unis d'Amérique, durant l'année 1818 | journal=Journal de Physique de Chimie et d'Histoire Naturelle et des Arts | volume=88 | pages=417–429 [418] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/6172336 }}{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2023 | title=New World warblers, mitrospingid tanagers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/warblers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=22 November 2023 }} The genus name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] ''helmins'' meaning "worm", and ''-thēras'' meaning "hunter". The specific epithet ''vermivorum'' is from [[Latin]] ''vermis'' meaning "worm" and ''-vorus'' meaning "-eating".{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=[https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n188/mode/1up 188], [https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n400/mode/1up 400]}} The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised. ==Description== The worm-eating warbler is a small [[New World warbler]]. It is around {{cvt|13|cm}} in length and weighs {{cvt|11.8|-|17.4|g}}.{{ cite book | last=Curson | first=J.M. | year=2010 | chapter=Family Parulidae (New World warblers) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Christie | editor3-first=D.A. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=15: Weavers to New World Warblers | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-96553-68-2| pages=666–800 [765] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0015unse/page/765/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }} It is relatively plain with olive-brown upperparts and light-coloured underparts, but has black and light brown stripes on its head. It has a slim pointed bill and pink legs. In immature birds, the head stripes are brownish. The male's song is a short high-pitched trill. This bird's call is a ''chip'' or ''{{not a typo|tseet}}''. Worm-eating warblers are [[Sex#Sexual monomorphism|sexually monomorphic]].{{cite book|last=Pyle|first=Peter|title=Identification guide to North American Birds|date=1997|publisher=Slate Creek Press|location=Bolinas, California|pages=499–500}} Males and females can only reliably be sexed during the breeding season by the presence of a [[brood patch]] in females or a [[cloacal protuberance]] in males. These birds are also difficult to age. Hatch year/second year birds have rusty tips on [[tertials]] that wear off by March of the following year. Juveniles can be distinguished by duskier head markings, and cinnamon wingbars. ==Distribution and habitat== These birds breed in the Eastern United States. Their selected habitats vary significantly between populations.{{cite journal|last=Watts|first=Bryan D.|author2=Michael D. Wilson |title=The use of pine plantations by worm-eating warblers in coastal North Carolina | journal=Southeastern Naturalist |date=2005| volume=4|issue=1|pages=177–187 |doi=10.1656/1528-7092(2005)004[0177:tuoppb]2.0.co;2}} In much of their range, worm-eating warblers are associated with mature hardwood forests on steep slopes. However, recent attention has been focused on coastal breeding populations, as little is known about their ecology or status. Historically, coastal populations would select for [[pocosin]] ecosystems. More recently, however, these populations have shifted to frequent use of pine plantations. Current use of pine plantations has resulted in densities higher than in areas previously thought to be their natural habitat. This shift in habitat selection likely demonstrates that worm-eating warblers are more closely associated with shrub structure than stand age or size. If this is the case, the landscape changes that occurred on the Atlantic coastal plain may have had less of an impact on these birds than previously described. Maintaining this species habitat may require managing for dense shrubby midstory and understory. Due to their reliance on shrub structure for foraging, and ground nesting behavior, frequent fires have a negative impact on this species.{{cite journal|last1=Artman|first1=Vanessa L.|first2=Elaine K.|last2=Sutherland|first3=Jerry F.|last3=Downhower|title=Prescribed burning to restore mixed-oak communities in southern Ohio: Effects on breeding bird populations | journal=Conservation Biology |date= 2008 |volume=15 |issue=5| pages=1423–1434|doi=10.1111/j.1523-1739.2001.00181.x}} Other management strategies that reduce the shrub mid-story, increase herbaceous growth, and decrease canopy cover are likely to have a similar effect. More information is needed about their breeding habits in coastal regions as these forests are likely to represent different conditions from their inland counterparts.{{cite journal|last=Pitts|first=Irvin|title=The Status and Breeding Habits of the Worm-eating Warbler in South Carolina|website=Carolinabirdclub.org|url=http://blogs.carolinabirdclub.org/chat/issues/1997/v61n2wewa.pdf|access-date=26 February 2014|archive-date=2 March 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140302134140/http://blogs.carolinabirdclub.org/chat/issues/1997/v61n2wewa.pdf|url-status=dead}} Fat deposits play a key role in allowing for long-distance migration in most passerines. Stopover habitat, or areas that allow birds to replenish their fat stores, are also critical.{{cite journal|last=Moore|first=F.|author2=P. Kerlinger |title=Stopover and fat deposition by North American wood warblers (Parulinae) following spring migration over the Gulf of Mexico|journal=Oecologia|date=1987|volume=74|issue=1|pages=47–54|doi=10.1007/bf00377344|pmid=28310413|bibcode=1987Oecol..74...47M|s2cid=3235707}} In winter, these birds [[bird migration|migrate]] to southern [[Mexico]], the [[Greater Antilles]], and [[Central America]] particularly along the [[Caribbean Slope]] where they occupy both scrub and moist forests.{{cite journal|last=Vitz|first=Andrew C.|author2=Lise A. Hanners |author3=Stephen R. Patton |title=Worm-eating Warbler (Helmitheros vermivorum)|journal=The Birds of North America Online|date=2013|doi=10.2173/bna.367|url=https://bna.birds.cornell.edu/bna/species/367|access-date=26 February 2014|url-access=subscription}} Worm-eating warblers have disappeared from some parts of their range due to habitat loss but their ability to use both scrub and moist forest ecosystems may be beneficial to the long term conservation of this species.{{cite journal|last=Robbins|first=C.S.|author2=J R Sauer |author3=R. S. Greenberg |author4=S. Droege |title=Population declines in North American birds that migrate to the neotropics|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|date=1989|volume=86|issue=19|pages=7658–7662|doi=10.1073/pnas.86.19.7658 |pmid=2798430|bibcode=1989PNAS...86.7658R|pmc=298126|doi-access=free}} ==Behaviour and ecology== ===Breeding=== This bird breeds in dense [[deciduous]] forests in the eastern United States, usually on wooded slopes. The nest is an [[cup nest|open cup]] placed on the ground, hidden among dead leaves. It is one of several species of new-world warblers that nests on the ground including the [[black-and-white warbler]] (''Mniotilta varia''), the [[ovenbird]] (''Seiurus aurocapilla''), the [[northern waterthrush]] (''Parkesia noveboracensis''), [[Louisiana waterthrush]] (''Parkesia motacilla''), [[palm warbler]] (''Setophaga palmarum''), and the [[Kentucky warbler]] (''Geothlypis formosa''). The female lays four or five eggs. Both parents feed the young; they may try to distract predators near the nest by [[Distraction display|pretending to be injured]]. Worm-eating warblers are often parasitized by [[brown-headed cowbird]]s (''Molothrus ater'') where [[Habitat fragmentation|forest fragmentation]] occurs.{{cite journal|last1=Robinson|first1=Scott K.|first2=Frank R.|last2=Thompson III|first3=Therese M.|last3=Donovan|first4=Donald R.|last4=Whitehead |first5=John|last5=Faaborg|title=Regional forest fragmentation and the nesting success of migratory birds|journal=Science|date=March 31, 1995|volume=267|issue=5206|pages=1987–1990|url=http://ww.prbo.org/cms/docs/terre/Robinsonetal1985science.pdf|access-date=26 February 2014|doi=10.1126/science.267.5206.1987|pmid=17770113|bibcode=1995Sci...267.1987R|s2cid=23144697}} Reducing forest fragmentation may prove vital if populations of worm-eating warblers suffer large declines. ===Food and feeding=== The diet of worm-eating warblers varies across habitat types.{{cite journal|last=Greenburg|first=Russell|title=Seasonal foraging specialization in the Worm-eating Warbler|journal=Condor|date=1987|volume=89|issue=1|pages=158–168|jstor=1368770|doi=10.2307/1368770}} This variation can be attributed to different predator avoidance strategies employed by common prey items. On their breeding grounds, worm-eating warblers glean mostly from live foliage, searching for [[arthropod]]s. On the wintering grounds, this species gleans insects almost exclusively from dead plant material. The name "worm-eating" refers to the numerous [[Lepidopteran]] [[larvae]] that this species consumes; they rarely if ever eat [[earthworm]]s. Use of pesticides, especially those broadcast over a wide area, is likely to have an effect on most insectivorous songbird species, including the worm-eating warbler.{{cite journal|last=Moulding|first=Jonathon|title=Effects of a low-persistence insecticide on forest bird populations|journal=The Auk|date=1976|volume=93|issue=4|pages=692–708|jstor=4084999}} These pesticides decrease the species' primary food source and could result in long-term toxicity. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[https://www.birds.cornell.edu/AllAboutBirds/BirdGuide/Worm-eating_Warbler.html Species account] - Cornell Lab of Ornithology *[https://www.mbr-pwrc.usgs.gov/id/framlst/i6390id.html Species account] - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification {{Passeroidea|E.|state=collapsed}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q269463}} [[Category:Parulidae|worm-eating warbler]] [[Category:Native birds of the Eastern United States]] [[Category:Birds of the Dominican Republic]] [[Category:Birds described in 1789|worm-eating warbler]] [[Category:Taxa named by Johann Friedrich Gmelin]]

zh.extract:
食虫森莺（台湾作食蟲鶯，学名：Helmitheros vermivorum），是雀形目森莺科的一种鸟类，属于单型的食虫森莺属（学名：Helmitheros）。
食虫森莺体重约14.16克，翼长约68.3毫米，喙宽度约3.7毫米，喙厚度约4.5毫米，嘴峰长约15.3毫米，跗蹠长约17.7毫米，尾长约48.4毫米。
食虫森莺是候鸟，栖息在森林，它们是食肉动物，主要以昆虫、蜘蛛等陆生无脊椎动物为食。它们分布在美国东部；冬季在墨西哥东南部至巴拿马和大安的列斯群岛越冬。
